speaker: Josh
text: Welcome to the 2026 Platforms State of the Union. This is one of our favorite moments of the year where we get to share what's new

speaker: Josh
text: with the technologies, the frameworks, and the tools that you use every day to build incredible apps and games.

speaker: Josh
text: Apps that inspire us, that raise the bar of what's possible and that push us to build even better technologies.

speaker: Josh
text: We love connecting with so many of you. Hearing about your passions, your challenges,

speaker: Josh
text: and how we can better support your work. Your feedback shapes some of the most important technologies that we build.

speaker: Josh
text: And this past year, nowhere was that more true than with the new design with Liquid Glass and with Apple Intelligence.

speaker: Josh
text: These were both huge themes in the 26 releases, and they're key again this year,

speaker: Josh
text: with many of our efforts influenced by your feedback. Design and intelligence are both so important because they enhance

speaker: Josh
text: what's special about your apps. The care and the craft that you put into them.

speaker: Josh
text: With unique interfaces and rich experiences shaped by your deep domain expertise.

speaker: Josh
text: Combined with enhanced intelligence capabilities, you can now build features that weren't previously possible.

speaker: Josh
text: To highlight what's new, we'll dive into three key areas. First, Apple Intelligence, with new ways to bring generative intelligence

speaker: Josh
text: directly into your apps, and new integrations with system intelligence to bring users back to your apps.

speaker: Josh
text: Second, platform improvements, with design refinements and more flexible UI layout,

speaker: Josh
text: updates to Swift and SwiftUI, and enhancements that make your apps faster,

speaker: Josh
text: more adaptive, and easier to build. And finally, developer productivity, taking agentic coding even further,

speaker: Josh
text: alongside improvements that make Xcode faster and more personal. We have a lot to cover, so let's get started with Apple Intelligence.

speaker: Josh
text: At the heart of Apple Intelligence are Apple Foundation models. Working together with Google and leveraging the technologies

speaker: Josh
text: behind their Gemini family of models, we created the latest Apple Foundation models

speaker: Josh
text: to power our Apple Intelligence experiences and toprovide even better support for the ways you're using intelligence in your apps.

speaker: Josh
text: We adapted these models to run on device and on Private Cloud Compute. Apple Foundation Models power Apple intelligence, and your apps can use them

speaker: Josh
text: too, through the Foundation Models framework. This year, the framework's capabilities are expanding to include image input and

speaker: Josh
text: support for server models. So if you have a more complex task requiring the most advanced frontier models

speaker: Josh
text: the API can now integrate with the cloud model provider of your choice. To ensure getting started with a large cloud model is as accessible as possible,

speaker: Josh
text: even if you're writing your first app, developers with fewer than 2 million first-time App Store downloads

speaker: Josh
text: will be able to use Apple Foundation Models running in Private Cloud Compute with no cloud API cost.

speaker: Josh
text: It's access to frontier level intelligence with unparalleled privacy protections.

speaker: Josh
text: Because getting started exploring ideas shouldn't be held back by infrastructure costs.

speaker: Josh
text: With these enhancements, the Foundation Models framework now offers a single API

speaker: Josh
text: that supports any model you need. In addition to features you build within your apps, Apple Intelligence can also

speaker: Josh
text: surface your app in more places across the system, giving users more ways to discover and return to it.

speaker: Josh
text: The App Intents framework connects your app to Apple Intelligence, drawing on core operating system technologies,

speaker: Josh
text: like the Spotlight semantic index. It organizes and surfaces personal context from any supported app.

speaker: Josh
text: The app toolbox, which identifies features available across apps to serve a user request.

speaker: Josh
text: And the system orchestrator, which coordinates it all while protecting user privacy.

speaker: Josh
text: Together, in-app and system-wide intelligence unlock experiences that neither could deliver alone.

speaker: Josh
text: Your apps made more powerful by intelligence. And intelligence made more meaningful by your apps.

speaker: Josh
text: Let's dive into these frameworks and see how they'll transform what your apps can do.

speaker: Richard
text: Here's Richard and Mary Beth. The Foundation Models framework is a native Swift API

speaker: Richard
text: that gives you direct access to the same on-device model that powers Apple intelligence. And many of you have already adopted it, creating experiences for shopping apps

speaker: Richard
text: like Wayfair, educational apps like CellWalk, local sports apps like CricHeroes and more,

speaker: Richard
text: all running on device with no infrastructure costs or privacy trade-offs. It's amazing to see how you've pushed the limits of what an on-device model can do.

speaker: Richard
text: We've got exciting updates for you. Let's start with a preview of the intelligence-powered features you will be able to build today.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Mary Beth, over to you. This year, we're building a sample app all about the Japanese paper craft of origami.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: It's a place to unwind and get creative with paper. I'll give you a quick tour. Our app starts with a beautiful gallery of my origami projects.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: And what's special about these is that I've used foundation models to tailor origami projects to match a person's interests and materials

speaker: Mary Beth
text: with step-by-step feedback. Of course, it's more fun to craft with friends. So our app has a built-in chat.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: It's a fun focused place to plan meetups and talk about crafting. Now I'm working on a cool feature to combine people's interests

speaker: Mary Beth
text: into an origami project. Here, I'll take this paper Rachel's bringing and mix in a photo of my dog

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to generate a fun project for us all to fold together. With Foundation Models framework, my app analyzes the inspiration pictures

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to get a sense of the materials I have and this dog theme I have in mind. It even translates the Japanese text and uses all of this context

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to brainstorm a few options. I'll choose this one. The intelligence keeps going in a fully interactive tutorial.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: That's a quick preview. Let's talk about the framework. This year, we're taking the Foundation Models framework

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to the next level. First, you get new capabilities like multimodal prompts with text and images.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: This opens up new categories of experiences you can build with image understanding.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: It's as simple as attaching an image to your prompt. In addition to this, the Vision framework is now integrated,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: giving you purpose-built tools the model can use, such as OCR for precise text extraction,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: and barcode readers for quick code scanning all on-device. Next, let's talk about server models.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: On-device models are incredibly useful for many tasks. Yet sometimes you might want a larger model for a more complex workflow.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: That's why we're extending the framework so you can easily call server models like Claude, Gemini, and more

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to use features like tool calling and guided generation. And any model provider can create a Swift package that conforms

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to the Language Model protocol. So you can pick the one you want for your app. In addition to this, we're opening up access for those of you

speaker: Mary Beth
text: getting started with AI to use the Apple Foundation Model running on Private Cloud Compute with no cloud API cost,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: giving you access to frontier-level intelligence while ensuring your user's data is not stored or accessible

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to Apple or anyone else. Your users will have access to features leveraging the cloud model every day,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: and iCloud+ subscribers will have expanded access. No matter the model you want to use, you can easily swap it in.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: This makes the Foundation Models framework the best way to run any large language model in your app.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: With more modalities and more models at your fingertips, the next thing you'll need is more ways to put them to work.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: That's why we're introducing a new open source Swift package, loaded with pre-built tools to help you get started

speaker: Mary Beth
text: with concepts like skills and utilities for context management. For example, a task management app like Tiimo can use the package

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to pull in a skill that adapts its tone and recommendations to the user's data,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: delivering a personalized brief to help them stay on top of their day. This space evolves so quickly.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Tomorrow's abstractions may be very different from today's. So these utilities from the open source package are created

speaker: Mary Beth
text: with new fundamental building blocks called Dynamic Profiles. These are new declarative APIs in the Foundation Models framework

speaker: Mary Beth
text: for building truly adaptive AI experiences with less code, so you can orchestrate skills and sub-agents,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: swap tools in and out, and update instructions on the fly.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: I'll walk you through how Dynamic Profiles powers intelligence in the Origami app. First, let's open Xcode.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: I'll start with a LanguageModelSession, which many of you already use. Now, instead of creating a session with a fixed model, tools, and instructions,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: with Dynamic Profiles, you have the freedom to continuously update your session. So I'll choose a profile for the LanguageModelSession

speaker: Mary Beth
text: and start with the familiar Swift result builder syntax. Here in the body, I'll define my first Profile as a brainstorming helper

speaker: Mary Beth
text: that's going to generate project ideas based on the photos I give it. I'll add modifiers to use Private Cloud Compute language model

speaker: Mary Beth
text: with temperature cranked up for creativity. Now the beauty of a Dynamic Profile is that this body will always resolve

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to just one Profile driving my session at a time, but I can switch between as many Profiles

speaker: Mary Beth
text: as my feature needs in the same session. So I'll change Profile based on my app state.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Then I can add in a second Profile to handle tutorial generation. Using Private Cloud Compute again with reasoning level set to deep,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: since this is my most challenging task. Last, I'll add in a Profile that explains any origami jargon, like "valley fold",

speaker: Mary Beth
text: that the user doesn't understand. This is a nice smaller task. I can send the on-device SystemLanguageModel

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to save on server calls. Let's see it in action. Here in my tutorial, I can now tap this term I don't understand,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: and the on-device model generates a nice explanation. In Dynamic Profiles, I'm swapping out models,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: but everything shares the same continuous transcript. This means more contextual intelligence with less prompting.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Now let's use that in my Tutorial Profile. Instructions and tools can be swapped in and out as well.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: So I'll use my app's view model to check if the tutorial's been generated, and if so, I'll add in instructions and tools to help the model

speaker: Mary Beth
text: give high-quality feedback tailored to the user. This body recomputes on every model turn, so my session will stay up to date.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Finally, do you see how my three Origami Profiles look a bit like three AI agents?

speaker: Mary Beth
text: That's because Dynamic Profiles are designed to be adaptable building blocks. So if you want to build AI agents or skills

speaker: Mary Beth
text: or any other high-level abstraction, you can. With the focus of flexibility and composability, these new APIs

speaker: Mary Beth
text: from the Foundation Models framework are here to grow with you. To help you bring all of this to your apps,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: you'll have access to a complete set of tools, from building to testing, to shipping with confidence.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: That includes the new Evaluations framework, which gives you the ability to test your prompts and validate

speaker: Mary Beth
text: that your intelligence-powered features work reliably. The upgraded Foundation Models instrument will help you visualize and debug

speaker: Mary Beth
text: model behavior in your apps. And the new FM command line tool lets you prompt the model right from the terminal.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: And there's so much more, like a Python SDK, tool calling with images, and a new RAG tool

speaker: Mary Beth
text: powered by Core Spotlight that's private to your app. That's the Foundation Models framework, providing you seamless access to

speaker: Mary Beth
text: multiple models with all new capabilities in the native Swift API. Plus, we're doing something big.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Later this summer, the framework will be open source. So the same Swift APIs you use in your app can now run on your server too,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: giving you a complete end-to-end AI workflow anywhere you deploy Swift. You've seen how the Foundation Models framework connects to third-party models,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Private Cloud Compute, and the on-device model. You have the flexibility you need to get the right model for the job.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: And when you want to bring a specific model into your app and run it on device, there's Core AI.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Core AI is a brand new framework built right into the platform, along with supporting tools and technologies.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: It's designed to be the best way to bring and run models on device in your apps.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: It delivers uncompromising performance through a modern memory-safe Swift API, with extensive tuning capabilities from fine-grained interest management

speaker: Mary Beth
text: and model specialization to custom GPU kernels. And there are Python-based tools alongside the framework, so you can convert

speaker: Mary Beth
text: and optimize your PyTorch models for the Core AI runtime. The framework is backed by deep integration

speaker: Mary Beth
text: into a new developer toolchain, with ahead-of-time compilation, dedicated Core AI instruments,

speaker: Mary Beth
text: and a powerful visual debugger to trace tensor values directly back to your original Python source code.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: And it's engineered to scale with your available compute. So you can run a compact vision model in your iPhone app

speaker: Mary Beth
text: for real-time camera queries. Or deploy a multi-billion parameter LLM in a Mac app right at your desk

speaker: Mary Beth
text: to power an agentic assistant for complex, multi-step workflows.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Whatever the device, whatever the model, it all runs on-device with zero server dependencies and zero token costs.

speaker: Mary Beth
text: Core AI is optimized for performance on Apple silicon, and it empowers Apple Intelligence experiences

speaker: Mary Beth
text: across the system, including Siri. And this year, Apple Intelligence offers even more opportunities for developers.

speaker: Lori
text: Over to Lori. Apple Intelligence draws on personal context from across apps,

speaker: Lori
text: understands what's on screen, and can take actions to get things done. And now you can integrate with its capabilities

speaker: Lori
text: through the App Intents framework. It's how our platforms understand what your apps can do. With it, you can make your app's content easier to find

speaker: Lori
text: and its capabilities easier to use through the Action button, Shortcuts, widgets, and in Siri AI.

speaker: Lori
text: App Intents schemas make integration with Siri's capabilities easy. Schemas are recognizable structures that Siri understands deeply,

speaker: Lori
text: built on years of language model training. We provide entity schemas for describing the content and concepts your app works with,

speaker: Lori
text: and intent schemas for describing the actions it can perform. Entity schemas enable personal context understanding.

speaker: Lori
text: By contributing your app's content to the Spotlight semantic index, you can help your users find information from your app quickly and easily

speaker: Lori
text: with attribution back to your app. The indexing keys for important content properties are built right in,

speaker: Lori
text: which means more understanding from less code. Siri's understanding of intent schemas means people can make requests naturally.

speaker: Lori
text: They don't need to learn specific phrases and you don't have to define them in your code. Schemas cover common app categories

speaker: Lori
text: like task management, photo editing, and communication, and include a whole set of system-supported actions.

speaker: Lori
text: Just adopt the relevant intent schemas for the actions your app can perform to make them available to your users.

speaker: Lori
text: And because these schemas are system-defined, they'll benefit from future updates. That means as Siri's language understanding evolves

speaker: Lori
text: or as we add new support for languages or regional dialects, your intents will work there too, without any changes to your code.

speaker: Lori
text: Combining these capabilities with the new View Annotations API will let your users reference and take action on the content in your app when it's on screen.

speaker: Lori
text: So your users can interact with your app conversationally, saying what feels natural to them, not commands they have to memorize.

speaker: Lori
text: Now, I'm going to show you how we've made our Origami app work with Siri. I already have some entities and intents that describe my app's content

speaker: Lori
text: and actions, and the entities conform to the IndexedEntity protocol, so they can be indexed into Spotlight.

speaker: Lori
text: By also conforming to an entity schema, Siri will be able to discover and reason over my app's content.

speaker: Lori
text: I'll make sure my Message, Contact, and Conversation entities all conform to the relevant entity schemas by using the @AppEntity macro.

speaker: Lori
text: I'm indexing these entities into Spotlight when my app finishes launching to make sure everything's in sync.

speaker: Lori
text: I've rebuilt to get the latest changes, and now let's see what I can do, even when I'm not in the app.

speaker: Lori
text: Hey Siri, who's coming to origami night?

speaker: Lori
text: Siri: Based on your messages in Origami, it looks like Kevin, Mary Beth, Rachel, and Richard are discussing origami night.

speaker: Lori
text: What's Richard bringing? Siri: Richard mentioned he is thinking of bringing pizza.

speaker: Lori
text: Awesome. But now I want to follow up, which means I need to act on this information.

speaker: Lori
text: I can make the content Siri found actionable by conforming an intent to the sendMessage schema.

speaker: Lori
text: This time I'm using the @AppIntent macro, since this is an action rather than content.

speaker: Lori
text: I'll build and run again, and now I can say, Siri, text Richard, "Can you make one of the pizzas vegetarian?"

speaker: Lori
text: Siri: From Origami: Ready to send it? Yes.

speaker: Lori
text: Siri: It's sent. Great. Message sent. I'd also like to let people reference what's on screen in my app

speaker: Lori
text: just by saying "the second message" or "this photo". The new View Annotations API lets me associate my views with entities,

speaker: Lori
text: which can then be passed to my app's intents, making them actionable. My Message List view contains all the individual messages in a conversation.

speaker: Lori
text: I can use a new view modifier to map each message row to its respective MessageEntity.

speaker: Lori
text: Let's try it out. Hey Siri, send this photo to Kevin and say,

speaker: Lori
text: Siri: From Origami: Ready to send it? Yes. Siri: It's sent.

speaker: Lori
text: And Siri sends the photo with my message. By combining personal context, common app actions,

speaker: Lori
text: and on-screen awareness, your app can become part of the intelligent fabric of the system. Through Siri, users can access it through natural language,

speaker: Josh
text: discover it through semantic search, and integrate it into their daily workflows. Now, back to Josh.

speaker: Josh
text: This is our vision for an intelligent platform. Rich, native experiences and intelligent natural language interfaces

speaker: Josh
text: working together. As app developers, this represents an incredible opportunity.

speaker: Josh
text: You can enhance your app's experiences through natural language with Siri, build powerful AI features with the Foundation Models framework,

speaker: Josh
text: and even run your own models on-device with Core AI. If you're using a custom model to power a feature within your app,

speaker: Josh
text: Core AI is the right technology to use. Your models will perform efficiently across all devices,

speaker: Josh
text: and it's built into the platform, so your apps always benefit from the latest fixes and enhancements.

speaker: Josh
text: And if you're an enthusiast who is experimenting with, training, researching, or fine-tuning generative models, or if you're running a local inference server,

speaker: Josh
text: our array framework, MLX, makes it easy to explore cutting-edge innovations and technologies.

speaker: Josh
text: It now supports Metal 4, GPU Neural Accelerators, and it can even scale

speaker: Josh
text: training across multiple Macs with RDMA over Thunderbolt. It's all open source and it's faster than ever.

speaker: Josh
text: The breadth of capabilities offered by Apple Intelligence and powered by Apple silicon makes Apple's platforms the best place to build and deliver

speaker: Josh
text: the next generation of intelligence-enhanced apps and games. Now let's take a look a level deeper at what makes all of this possible -

speaker: Josh
text: the systems your apps depend on, from the frameworks you call to the processes that schedule your work, manage your memory, and render your UI.

speaker: Josh
text: We took an especially close look at the performance and quality of these foundations, and you'll see a multitude of platform improvements

speaker: Josh
text: in this year's releases. When you rebuild with the new SDK, your apps will launch faster

speaker: Josh
text: and feel more responsive. You'll see refinements and platform improvements across frameworks media,

speaker: Josh
text: search, and accessibility, enhancements to Swift and SwiftUI, and a lot more,

speaker: Josh
text: especially around design. Last year, the new design with Liquid Glass

speaker: Josh
text: brought a unified design language built for a world where your experience moves across devices.

speaker: Josh
text: The new design is making apps more expressive and delightful while staying instantly familiar to users.

speaker: Josh
text: It looks great in apps like Tide Guide, where subtle, interactive highlights respond as users scroll through tide data and charts.

speaker: Josh
text: And SketchPro, where translucent brush panels and controls let artwork show through, even while you switch between tools.

speaker: Josh
text: Throughout the last year, we've been refining the design, and that journey continues with a new set of design updates in the 27 releases.

speaker: Josh
text: Apps that have already adopted Liquid Glass benefit from many of these improvements automatically.

speaker: Josh
text: To tell you more, here's Cindy. In this year's releases, you'll see updates to the foundations

speaker: Josh
text: of how Liquid Glass is built, refinements to the new design that improve consistency, and new ways for iOS apps

speaker: Josh
text: to adapt across devices and screen sizes. Let's review the changes you'll see in your apps.

speaker: Josh
text: To maintain exceptional readability, we tuned Liquid Glass so it more effectively diffuses complex content behind it.

speaker: Josh
text: And to establish more depth and separation, we also introduced a darkened edge along with brighter specular highlights.

speaker: Josh
text: We also made it more personalizable with a new slider in settings to adjust Liquid Glass anywhere from ultra clear to fully tinted,

speaker: Josh
text: allowing users to choose the look that works best for them. Apps already using Liquid Glass get these improvements automatically

speaker: Josh
text: when they run on this year's releases without even needing to recompile. Liquid Glass seamlessly adapts to a variety of accessibility settings

speaker: Josh
text: users may choose, such as reducing transparency or increasing contrast.

speaker: Josh
text: And now macOS 27 also supports the "show borders" environment value, just like iOS.

speaker: Josh
text: So you can adapt your macOS app's custom controls for this setting as well. Sidebars expand to the edges on Mac and iPad, providing clearer structure

speaker: Josh
text: while still refracting content from your app and the wallpaper. And icons in the sidebar regain their color using your app's accent color,

speaker: Josh
text: giving your app more personality and making it more clear which window is key. List and Label APIs provide these updates automatically

speaker: Josh
text: and support customizing the tint per item. And every window on macOS now also has the same tighter corner radius,

speaker: Josh
text: ensuring greater consistency across all apps. When content scrolls under floating bars, a uniform toolbar appears across the top

speaker: Josh
text: and keeps the text legible while improving contrast. This effect is applied automatically for standard toolbars

speaker: Josh
text: and can be customized using the existing scroll edge effect APIs. We also thought about how icons and menus can be used intentionally

speaker: Josh
text: to call attention to the most important actions, both on macOS and iPadOS.

speaker: Josh
text: While icons are hidden by default, there's an API to show icons for key app actions.

speaker: Josh
text: We're also updating how Liquid Glass shows up in icons, making them sharper and more defined.

speaker: Josh
text: This updated rendering applies to all app icons, and we've introduced new features such as refraction

speaker: Josh
text: that can be selectively used for added character. And with Icon Composer, you can now design your icons

speaker: Josh
text: out of multiple layers of Liquid Glass. It's been updated with new annotation features to add refraction

speaker: Josh
text: or dial in Liquid Glass content effects. And it provides an interactive preview of how your icon will look

speaker: Josh
text: on earlier releases. Together, these updates culminate in a more focused and approachable experience

speaker: Josh
text: across your apps and across platforms. Next, let's talk about app adaptability.

speaker: Josh
text: iOS apps show up in more places than ever, on iPad as an iPhone app, or on Mac through iPhone Mirroring.

speaker: Josh
text: When your iOS app shows up in these other contexts with larger displays, users want to be able to take advantage of the extra space

speaker: Josh
text: to see more information. So this year we're introducing support to resize iOS apps

speaker: Josh
text: in iPhone Mirroring and on iPad. Let's see how this works with the Origami app.

speaker: Josh
text: Once you rebuild with the latest SDK, your app is automatically opted in to resizability.

speaker: Josh
text: Since Origami is a SwiftUI app, it's already taking advantage of scene lifecycle and standard framework support for basic resizability.

speaker: Josh
text: If you're already using SwiftUI, Auto Layout, or responding to size class changes, you are well on your way

speaker: Josh
text: to supporting full resizability. If you have custom views, you'll want to update them to using auto layout

speaker: Josh
text: and trait collections for layout decisions. Using the new resizable iOS simulator and Previews,

speaker: Josh
text: you can test across a variety of screen sizes right in Xcode, so you'll see exactly how your layout performs.

speaker: Josh
text: And we're providing a skill for coding agents that will help you find and fix common resizability issues.

speaker: Josh
text: Now, instead of designing for specific devices and orientations, you're designing for a dynamic range of sizes and aspect ratios.

speaker: Josh
text: To provide the best experience when using iPhone Mirroring, update your app to be able to adapt and support any size.

speaker: Josh
text: Resizable simulator, Previews, and iPhone Mirroring all make it easy to ensure your app is as dynamic and flexible as possible.

speaker: Franck
text: Next, let's talk about SwiftUI. Here's Franck. SwiftUI is the best way to build apps for any Apple device.

speaker: Franck
text: We designed SwiftUI to capture everything we know about building great apps on our platforms.

speaker: Franck
text: It gracefully handles the complexities of layout, animation, and platform integration

speaker: Franck
text: so you can focus on what makes your app yours. And as new capabilities like Liquid Glass are added, apps get these features easily

speaker: Franck
text: because they're designed with SwiftUI in mind. New apps like The Goat are built with SwiftUI

speaker: Franck
text: because they want to feel truly at home on Apple platforms. The Goat is a game development environment that brings the open source Godot engine

speaker: Franck
text: to Apple devices. It started on iPad, expanded to iPhone, and when the time came to bring it to Mac,

speaker: Franck
text: it felt completely natural. And apps that previously used cross-platform or web technologies

speaker: Franck
text: like Notion are migrating their user interface to SwiftUI because they want a level of performance and UI consistency

speaker: Franck
text: that other technologies can't deliver. With powerful agentic coding tools, porting code to Swift

speaker: Franck
text: has never been easier. Of course, we reach for SwiftUI ourselves whenever we build apps.

speaker: Franck
text: For example, SwiftUI made it easy to build a new Siri app by enabling us

speaker: Franck
text: to share code across all our platforms and Creator Studio apps like Logic Pro,

speaker: Franck
text: build new features with SwiftUI for high performance and cross-platform support.

speaker: Franck
text: Since we rely on SwiftUI ourselves, every improvement we make for our own apps

speaker: Franck
text: becomes an improvement for your apps too. And this is a big year for SwiftUI.

speaker: Franck
text: With richer interactions that help you write less custom code, with speed making your apps much faster,

speaker: Franck
text: and finally, with new capabilities for your apps. Let's start with interactions.

speaker: Franck
text: This year, SwiftUI brings more dynamic interactions to your app like reorderable containers, which makes it super easy

speaker: Franck
text: to add drag to reorder to any container. Building a grid reordering experience outside of lists, like with this grid

speaker: Franck
text: in the Origami app, used to require a lot of code. Now, it is just as simple as adding .reorderable() to your ForEach

speaker: Franck
text: and .reorderContainer() to the parent. And just like that, I can customize the order of my Origami models.

speaker: Franck
text: SwiftUI handles the lift and the drop animations and it works with any container

speaker: Franck
text: like grids and stacks. Now, for Origami models I feel a little less proud of,

speaker: Franck
text: SwiftUI now supports swipe actions inside any container as well. I can delete a custom row with a swipe

speaker: Franck
text: by adding the existing .swipeActions() modifier to my row and .swipeActionsContainer() to the scrollable container.

speaker: Franck
text: This provides great flexibility for quick actions on my custom row. Finally, text selection got more flexible too.

speaker: Franck
text: On iOS, it gains the same. full-fidelity selection already found in TextField and TextEditor.

speaker: Franck
text: And on macOS, it now supports custom text renderers, text vibrancy,

speaker: Franck
text: and vertical text. Next, let's take a look at speed. This is always a priority for us, but even more so this year,

speaker: Franck
text: and you will see many improvements without any changes on your end. To start, we've been gradually unifying the architectures of SwiftUI, AppKit,

speaker: Franck
text: and UIKit, and this year, they share a common foundation across many controls.

speaker: Franck
text: So wherever your app is running, they can benefit from the same on-the-line improvements.

speaker: Franck
text: For example, menu pickers on macOS are now better equipped to smoothly handle large lists of items.

speaker: Franck
text: And in nested stack layouts, whereas SwiftUI used to measure each child multiple times

speaker: Franck
text: to resolve their flexibility, it now short-circuits computations where they're not needed,

speaker: Franck
text: meaning layouts now resize up to twice as fast. And nothing saves performance like avoiding unnecessary work.

speaker: Franck
text: SwiftUI now only initializes state objects when they're first loaded.

speaker: Franck
text: Previously, a new temporary instance of the state object would get created

speaker: Franck
text: every time the view is reinitialized. You get this improvement for free because state is now lazy under the hood

speaker: Franck
text: and was converted from a dynamic property to a macro. And when it comes to loading images, AsyncImage avoids redundancies as well.

speaker: Franck
text: It now caches its content automatically using standard HTTP caching,

speaker: Franck
text: so images are downloaded once and only re-fetched when needed. Finally, let's talk about new capabilities starting with toolbars.

speaker: Franck
text: With the new resizability features, optimizing your app for a dynamic range of sizes and aspect ratios is more important than ever.

speaker: Franck
text: And toolbars are central to that experience. This year, SwiftUI gives you finer control over how toolbar items adapt to space.

speaker: Franck
text: Use the new visibilityPriority modifier to mark your most important items high.

speaker: Franck
text: And SwiftUI keeps them visible longer as space shrinks. Less prominent actions, like archive or delete, can be added to

speaker: Franck
text: the new toolbars overflow menu container, which groups them in an overflow menu.

speaker: Franck
text: And finally, the new topBarPinnedTrailing placement anchors items to the trailing edge, no matter how the toolbar reflows.

speaker: Franck
text: Now, when I resize the window, the toolbar stays organized exactly how I want.

speaker: Franck
text: Important buttons stay visible. Deprioritized ones are in the overflow menu and share is always pinned

speaker: Franck
text: to the trailing edge. Tabs can also be distinguished with the new prominent tab role

speaker: Franck
text: pinning the tab to the trailing edge of the screen. SwiftUI also opens up new ground for document-based apps

speaker: Franck
text: with a new document infrastructure that provides a ton of functionality

speaker: Franck
text: out of the box, like first-class URL access for fully customizable reading and writing to disk,

speaker: Franck
text: the kind that powers apps like Xcode or Pages. For example, with direct access to the file URL, you now have the flexibility

speaker: Franck
text: to read just the parts of a file you need and write only the pieces that changed,

speaker: Franck
text: not the entire file. You can also observe and update document attributes

speaker: Franck
text: using the provided observable configuration. The new document API integrates deeply with modern Swift,

speaker: Franck
text: with support for observation, Swift concurrency, and so much more.

speaker: Franck
text: Lastly, here is something pretty awesome. The Spatial Preview framework gives Mac apps new ways

speaker: Franck
text: to extend in space around users wearing Apple Vision Pro. When you adopt this new API in your app, a 3D model can become spatial

speaker: Franck
text: when you stream to Apple Vision Pro, allowing your users to preview, edit,

speaker: Franck
text: and share objects and models in real time. Beyond those we've mentioned already, there are many other new improvements,

speaker: Franck
text: including better type checking performance with content builders, a new alert binding API, and to support adjusting cross-fade transitions.

speaker: Franck
text: Together, these platform improvements bring more speed, richer interactions, and

speaker: Franck
text: powerful new capabilities to SwiftUI that you can take advantage of. Now, let's take a look at Swift itself.

speaker: Franck
text: Here's Holly. Swift is designed to be the language you reach for at every layer of the stack.

speaker: Holly
text: Whether you're building full-featured mobile apps, internet-scale services, or embedded firmware, Swift helps you write code that's fast, expressive, and safe.

speaker: Holly
text: Swift's performance, depth, and unmatched interoperability make it the natural

speaker: Holly
text: successor to C and C++ for low-level systems and server programming.

speaker: Holly
text: And its approachability and expressiveness make it ideal for higher-level development like apps and frameworks.

speaker: Holly
text: We think Swift is the only language with this breadth. That's what makes Swift a language you can keep reaching for as your stack grows.

speaker: Holly
text: Including outside Apple platforms, where the tools for development on Linux, Windows, Android, and the web are available on Swift.org.

speaker: Holly
text: Many of you are extending your use of Swift to additional platforms and the server so you can reuse code and benefit from Swift's high performance

speaker: Holly
text: across your entire stack, like Flighty, which uses Swift in their services to share the code that tracks airport visits

speaker: Holly
text: between the app and backend. Or GoodNotes, which uses Swift for WebAssembly to bring the app to the web,

speaker: Holly
text: Chrome OS, Android, and Windows, reusing over 100,000 lines of code.

speaker: Holly
text: Or Frameo, which uses Swift-Java interoperability to share Swift libraries between the iOS app and the PhotoFrame software written in Java.

speaker: Holly
text: Interoperability means you can bring Swift into C, C++, and Java systems

speaker: Holly
text: you already have. So you can get Swift's benefits at every layer without a rewrite.

speaker: Holly
text: At Apple, we've been building with Swift at every layer of our own stack.

speaker: Holly
text: Foundation paved the way for Objective-C frameworks to move to native Swift under the hood.

speaker: Holly
text: AppKit and UIKit have followed suit by using Swift and SwiftUI extensively in their implementation.

speaker: Holly
text: WebKit, the open source web engine that powers Safari, is a large and security-critical C++ code base.

speaker: Holly
text: Using Swift's safe C++ interoperability, WebKit is replacing core components

speaker: Holly
text: with Swift versions incrementally. In the networking stack, the QUIC transport layer was rewritten in Swift.

speaker: Holly
text: Later this month, the project will be open sourced and available for cross-platform use hrough SwiftNIO integration.

speaker: Holly
text: You can follow along or get involved through the vibrant open source community on Swift.org.

speaker: Holly
text: Further down the stack, more security and performance critical systems moved to Swift this year.

speaker: Holly
text: The TrueType font rendering engine replaced decades of hand-optimized C with Swift code that's not only memory safe, but also faster.

speaker: Holly
text: At the lowest level, we've written hundreds of thousands of lines of Swift code across bare metal firmware, coprocessors, and drivers.

speaker: Holly
text: For the 27 releases, we've started writing parts of the core operating system kernel

speaker: Holly
text: in Swift. As Swift becomes more capable in these domains, we're staying true

speaker: Holly
text: to one of Swift's most important design goals: it's fun.

speaker: Holly
text: It's natural to write and iterate on your ideas in Swift, and the compiler is there to catch mistakes along the way.

speaker: Holly
text: The latest updates are focused on improving your workflow so you can focus on the fun part:

speaker: Holly
text: writing great code with confidence. Swift 6.4 is here, built to make everyday tasks feel effortless.

speaker: Holly
text: I'll show you just a few examples. When your code base is undergoing a migration or incremental adoption

speaker: Holly
text: of new features, sometimes it's not realistic to address all compiler warnings across your project at once.

speaker: Holly
text: You can now suppress warnings in specific parts of your code, and you can promote warnings to errors in places where you want strict enforcement.

speaker: Holly
text: Availability attributes can get long and repetitive when you're writing code for multiple Apple platforms.

speaker: Holly
text: Now, instead of listing out every Apple platform with the same version number, you can simply write 'anyAppleOS'.

speaker: Holly
text: The limitation on async calls in a defer block is gone, and awaiting inside a defer just works.

speaker: Holly
text: No matter what you're building, Swift's compiler diagnostics are a daily companion, helping you catch mistakes early

speaker: Holly
text: and guiding you toward correct code. If you've spent time writing Swift code, you've probably encountered this error message:

speaker: Holly
text: or in deeply nested SwiftUI view bodies. This is frustrating, and we've made it a lot better.

speaker: Holly
text: In many common cases, code that hit this fallback error will now either compile successfully or give you a more actionable error to work with.

speaker: Holly
text: We know this area is important for a smooth workflow, and we're continuing to invest in it.

speaker: Holly
text: Swift 6.4 makes you more productive in day-to-day code, and it brings that same care to the more specialized corners

speaker: Holly
text: of your project. There's never been a better time to go full stack with Swift. Now back to Josh.

speaker: Josh
text: So those are the platform improvements in this year's releases. Now, to move ahead, sometimes we have to leave something behind.

speaker: Josh
text: As we said last year, macOS Tahoe was the final release to support Intel Macs.

speaker: Josh
text: The transition of macOS to Apple silicon is now complete, enabling us to focus

speaker: Josh
text: on a single architecture across the entire ecosystem. This can benefit your apps as well.

speaker: Josh
text: You can now ship Apple silicon-only binaries on the Mac App Store, reducing your app's download size and letting you focus your testing

speaker: Josh
text: on a single architecture. And with all the refinements to the new design with Liquid Glass,

speaker: Josh
text: it's time to complete your migration there too. We'll be removing support for opting to use the old design.

speaker: Josh
text: So once your app is recompiled with Xcode 27, it will automatically begin to use the new design with Liquid Glass.

speaker: Josh
text: With so many improvements across the system, your apps and games will look and feel better than ever on this year's releases.

speaker: Josh
text: Now, let's turn to your productivity and the tools you use to build with and for our platforms.

speaker: Josh
text: Intelligence is deeply transforming how you write code, add new features, and build apps.

speaker: Josh
text: Last year, we brought AI coding assistance to Xcode, and so many of you embraced it immediately.

speaker: Josh
text: It's helping you write code faster and adopt new APIs more easily. This space moves really fast, so we've picked up the pace of our releases,

speaker: Josh
text: delivering new Xcode capabilities to you faster than ever before. Earlier this year, we brought coding agents to Xcode, along with tools allowing

speaker: Josh
text: agents to grab a preview, search documentation, and more, powered by the Model Context Protocol.

speaker: Josh
text: With MCP, Xcode also connects to the tools you already use, from design apps like Figma to services like GitHub.

speaker: Josh
text: And Xcode includes a built-in integration for agents from Anthropic, OpenAI,

speaker: Josh
text: and now Google. Today, Xcode adds support for Agent Client Protocol,

speaker: Josh
text: so you can bring any compatible agent into Xcode. ACP support and Gemini integration

speaker: Josh
text: are shipping in an update to Xcode 26 available today. And there's more coming in Xcode 27.

speaker: Ken
text: Here's Ken. From the first line of code to the App Store, Xcode is where you build the best apps for Apple platforms.

speaker: Ken
text: Millions of you live in it for writing and debugging your code with coding agents right alongside,

speaker: Ken
text: designing interfaces in SwiftUI and previewing them in real time, testing across devices and simulators to catch issues before your users do,

speaker: Ken
text: and profiling performance with instruments, keeping your apps fast, responsive, and efficient.

speaker: Ken
text: This year, Xcode has two big stories. The first is intelligence, and we have a lot to talk about in a minute.

speaker: Ken
text: The second is the daily experience, how Xcode feels to use.

speaker: Ken
text: Now just like you, we spend hours in Xcode every day. We build all our operating systems and apps with it.

speaker: Ken
text: In fact, we build Xcode with Xcode. So it needs to feel like home while being fast, fun, and personal.

speaker: Ken
text: And we've heard your feedback and improved Xcode across the board. It's faster at loading projects.

speaker: Ken
text: We fixed top crashes and spins. Debug sessions are more reliable, with faster expression evaluation,

speaker: Ken
text: and a console that can handle more intensive logging without hitching. Xcode 27 is 30% smaller.

speaker: Ken
text: Now, Apple silicon-only, with agents, documentation, and other components downloading in the background,

speaker: Ken
text: so you're always up to date. Now, let's take a look at the experience. First, your Xcode settings are now automatically saved to iCloud.

speaker: Ken
text: When I'm setting up a new Mac like this, Xcode offers to import them. I'll pull in the settings from my iMac.

speaker: Ken
text: I can sign in with my Apple ID. Xcode fills in my Git config too. And just like that, I'm ready to code with my new Mac.

speaker: Ken
text: Now, let's create a new project. Watch this. I'll select new project, then app and boom, I'm in the editor.

speaker: Ken
text: No file name, no bundle ID, no setup. Of course I can specify all those things later when I'm ready.

speaker: Ken
text: This is great for exploring an idea, a new API, or prototyping a view.

speaker: Ken
text: Alright, now I'll open the Origami project. Xcode 27 looks beautiful with the design refinements of macOS 27.

speaker: Ken
text: Crisp and clean. Let's customize it. In Xcode 27, you can make the toolbar your own.

speaker: Ken
text: It's easy to rearrange things, so I can add what I need and remove what I don't.

speaker: Ken
text: The activity view is now tucked neatly into the document title over here, so there's even more room for the things I want.

speaker: Ken
text: The navigation buttons, canvas toggle, editor splits, they're all right up here on the toolbar.

speaker: Ken
text: Now I'll add a shortcut to quickly create a new coding assistant conversation and that'll be useful a little bit later.

speaker: Ken
text: Next and super fun, themes. Color now flows throughout the entire app, not just the editor.

speaker: Ken
text: You can personalize everything from the background to syntax colors and dial in that perfect shade of purple for your keywords.

speaker: Ken
text: And Xcode 27 comes with gorgeous new choices. Let me show you a few of my favorites.

speaker: Ken
text: Emerald. That feels fresh. You can almost smell it. How about something with a little bit more energy?

speaker: Ken
text: Neon Noir. Electric. Love it. Light or dark? Every theme supports both.

speaker: Ken
text: Here's Coral Reef. I feel relaxed already. And when I'm working on multiple projects at the same time,

speaker: Ken
text: I can set a different theme for each. Makes it super easy to tell them apart at a glance.

speaker: Ken
text: All right, let's get back to work. Next, Xcode Cloud, which gives you continuous integration and delivery

speaker: Ken
text: built right into Xcode. I'll set up my Origami project to use it. I'll click get started, grant access to my repository, and that's it.

speaker: Ken
text: I can kick off my first cloud build. No App Store Connect setup needed.

speaker: Ken
text: And Xcode Cloud builds are up to twice as fast, now supporting Apple Vision Pro and apps using Metal on Apple silicon.

speaker: Ken
text: Next, Previews. They are the best way to iterate on UI and the easiest way to see

speaker: Ken
text: how your views look across variants, like accessibility sizes, orientations, and localizations.

speaker: Ken
text: And now you can see variations for any property. I'll open this view here that shows a craft note.

speaker: Ken
text: It renders differently based on the CraftState enum, which has four different values.

speaker: Ken
text: Now, I can pass that enum to the preview and just like that - I get a grid showing all the states of my UI.

speaker: Ken
text: All four in one glance. Next, another one I'm excited about.

speaker: Ken
text: When testing your app, you use real hardware to evaluate performance, use sensors, and test real-world conditions.

speaker: Ken
text: And you use simulators to cover older OSes and devices that you don't have.

speaker: Ken
text: Xcode 27 brings both together in the new Device Hub.

speaker: Ken
text: It replaces Simulator and it does a whole lot more too. Let me show you.

speaker: Ken
text: When I first run my app, the window looks like the Simulator I know. I can easily rotate, grab a screenshot and jump back to the home screen,

speaker: Ken
text: just like I'm used to. When I extend the view, I can now change device properties

speaker: Ken
text: and test how my app responds to different system settings. Like switching to dark mode, increasing the font size, and more.

speaker: Ken
text: We rebuilt the experience from the ground up for the highest fidelity possible.

speaker: Ken
text: So I can pinch to zoom, use two-finger scrolling, and like in Previews, I can dynamically resize the simulator to see how my iOS app

speaker: Ken
text: handles different sizes. I can also manage and interact with physical devices from the same place,

speaker: Ken
text: like this iPhone here on my desk. I'll launch the Origami app right here from my Mac.

speaker: Ken
text: And I can interact with it. All the convenience of the simulator with the fidelity of real hardware

speaker: Ken
text: in a single place. That is a quick look at the experience in Xcode 27.

speaker: Ken
text: And beyond the experience, the biggest changes this year, the ones that will truly accelerate you, are in intelligence.

speaker: Kevin
text: Kevin, over to you. What a great time to be a developer. Intelligence is transforming how you build apps.

speaker: Kevin
text: Agentic coding, together with Apple platforms, frameworks, and tools, helps you bring ideas to life.

speaker: Kevin
text: Xcode 27 takes the next big step in agentic coding, leveraging the full power

speaker: Kevin
text: of the best models and agents directly into Xcode. Agents are woven into every layer of the Xcode experience,

speaker: Kevin
text: from the way you interact with them to a set of tools that help you get the best results. Tools like understanding your project, searching documentation,

speaker: Kevin
text: building, and testing. And Xcode 27 helps you even more with new tools

speaker: Kevin
text: like rendering previews with variants, interacting with the simulator, localizing your app, debugging, and more.

speaker: Kevin
text: And this goes beyond tools. When using agents in Xcode, every answer is grounded in Swift,

speaker: Kevin
text: SwiftUI, in Apple frameworks. That's why, when building for Apple platforms,

speaker: Kevin
text: Xcode is the best place to code with agents. Let me show you what this looks like across every stage of app development.

speaker: Kevin
text: From starting with an idea to implementing and validating it, to improving it, like adding new languages or fixing issues.

speaker: Kevin
text: First, I'm gonna add something fun to the Origami app. My daughter, she loves making origami with me.

speaker: Kevin
text: I want to surprise her with a feature that makes up a little choose-your-own-adventure story about the characters that we make together.

speaker: Kevin
text: I'll start with a new conversation with the agent. I'll create one here from the toolbar. It opens right in the editor, just like any other file.

speaker: Kevin
text: Here's what I want to build. In my Origami project, I want a button that generates a choose-your-own-adventure story

speaker: Kevin
text: for my daughter. That alone would get me good results, but I had something a little more specific

speaker: Kevin
text: in mind, so I'll add some more details. She'll start by picking some options, like the setting and an item to use

speaker: Kevin
text: in the story. The app will generate the first page, and then let her pick what comes next.

speaker: Kevin
text: I want to use the latest Foundation Models APIs and present it with beautiful typography.

speaker: Kevin
text: When using a coding agent, the best results come from collaborating on the implementation and design first,

speaker: Kevin
text: before any code is written. So I'll add /plan to the prompt. And I'm gonna ask for a diagram while I'm at it.

speaker: Kevin
text: I find it easier to review the plan that way. Let's get this started. The agent is exploring my project using Xcode tools to help it efficiently

speaker: Kevin
text: understand my code base, its architecture and patterns, to find the best way to build the feature, and it's asking some clarifying questions.

speaker: Kevin
text: Do I want to persist it? Yes. And how many options for the next part of the story?

speaker: Kevin
text: Two to three is good. And the agent continues to create the plan. Let's skip forward in time.

speaker: Kevin
text: My plan is now ready, and it shows right next to the conversation in beautifully rendered markdown.

speaker: Kevin
text: It's very easy to review, and I can also refine it. Here, the current implementation has a fixed set of settings and items to choose.

speaker: Kevin
text: I'd love if she could add her own. I'll add that as a comment. And now the plan looks good.

speaker: Kevin
text: Let's kick it off. Now Xcode and the agent work together to implement the plan. Xcode shows everything that's changing, like code and previews.

speaker: Kevin
text: As it runs, I can refine the implementation. Like here, I'm gonna add a fun image filter to the story's hero image.

speaker: Kevin
text: It looks like Xcode is done building my feature. There's still a lot of code, and the previews look great.

speaker: Kevin
text: Let's just run it. I'll open an Origami project, tap the new toolbar icon, and just like we asked,

speaker: Kevin
text: page one offers an item and a setting. I'll pick a forest and a wand,

speaker: Kevin
text: and oh look, there's the button if I wanted to add my own. And here's the first page of the story with that gorgeous hero image.

speaker: Kevin
text: My daughter's gonna love this. What was just an idea a couple minutes ago is now something I can run in my app.

speaker: Kevin
text: This is amazing! Now, building a feature is more than writing code. It's also making sure it does what it's supposed to do.

speaker: Kevin
text: Xcode 27 can help you with that too, with new tools for agents to check their work.

speaker: Kevin
text: For example, agents can validate the logic of your app by running tests, try ideas in isolation using playgrounds, like experimenting with APIs,

speaker: Kevin
text: and check visual changes with Previews in light and dark mode, different orientations, text sizes, or localizations.

speaker: Kevin
text: And now agents can interact with your app in the simulator. Let me show you.

speaker: Kevin
text: I want to test different combinations of settings, items, even customized ones.

speaker: Kevin
text: Xcode launches Origami and Device Hub and starts testing those for me. The agent can tap, swipe, and type.

speaker: Kevin
text: When it's done, I get a summary of the tests, and I can see all the screenshots

speaker: Kevin
text: it created along the way. And just like that, the Origami app has a new story feature, designed, built,

speaker: Kevin
text: and tested end-to-end. Next, let's see how we can use agentic coding to improve our app.

speaker: Kevin
text: Agents in Xcode can help with all kinds of engineering tasks, like adopting new APIs,

speaker: Kevin
text: making your app more accessible, and more. Let's localize our Origami app. I'll start with French.

speaker: Kevin
text: Xcode automatically adds a new language to the strings catalog, then works with the agents to translate strings

speaker: Kevin
text: across the entire project. This is more than a word-for-word translation.

speaker: Kevin
text: Xcode looks at each string in its context, the surrounding code, UI, the action, to find the best translation.

speaker: Kevin
text: And when it's done, I can see all the translations here in the String Catalog.

speaker: Kevin
text: Let's build and run. My app is now localized! Fantastique!

speaker: Kevin
text: Building a great app also means responding to the feedback and data from your users.

speaker: Kevin
text: The Organizer already gives you insights into how your app is doing in the real world - crashes, hangs, performance metrics,

speaker: Kevin
text: anonymized and aggregated. Now, you can use agents to help you find issues that matter most, and fix them.

speaker: Kevin
text: I'll ask Xcode to pull up the top crashes from the latest release. I get a list of crashes ranked by how often they happen.

speaker: Kevin
text: Oh, that first one is from an update I pushed last week. Let's fix it.

speaker: Kevin
text: Xcode looks at the symbolicated crash log, figures out where in my project this happens, identifies the issue, reproduces the crash, makes the fix,

speaker: Kevin
text: and then validates it. And just like that, our issue is fixed.

speaker: Kevin
text: That's agents at work with Xcode across every stage of your development, planning, building, and improving.

speaker: Kevin
text: You'll be amazed when you see Xcode and agents bring your ideas to life. Finally, let's talk about what makes all of this possible.

speaker: Kevin
text: Xcode 27 ships with the expertise of Apple's engineers and designers built right in as a corpus of skills, documentation, and MCP tools.

speaker: Kevin
text: Think of them as specialists. A SwiftUI specialist that knows how to structure your view and data flow.

speaker: Kevin
text: An accessibility specialist that knows what makes an interface work for everyone. Specialists for universal sizing, testing, and performance.

speaker: Kevin
text: In fact, so much of what we've seen to this point is powered by one of these specialists. And you can bring your own!

speaker: Kevin
text: Xcode integrates all of them in the same way. Plugins. It's a format used by many agents and widely adopted by the community.

speaker: Kevin
text: It's amazing to see how many of these you have already been building and sharing. A plugin can contain skills, just markdown files that teach the agent new tasks.

speaker: Kevin
text: It can contain tools using the Model Context Protocol. And we've added one new capability to plugins.

speaker: Kevin
text: With the Agent Client Protocol, a plugin can bring an agent of your choice. Installing one is easy.

speaker: Kevin
text: You can use the command line or paste a git URL right into Xcode. And partners like Figma and GitHub make it even easier to set up with just one click.

speaker: Kevin
text: And then I can put it all together. I can tell Xcode to implement a Figma design in SwiftUI, refine it for different

speaker: Kevin
text: variants, make it resizable using a skill, and post a PR to GitHub. How cool is that?

speaker: Kevin
text: That's Agentic Coding in Xcode 27. From an idea to an app, at every step.

speaker: Kevin
text: Built for how you create, refine, and ship, and reach your users wherever they are.

speaker: Kevin
text: We can't wait to see what great things you make next! Back to you, Josh.

speaker: Josh
text: It's a huge year for Xcode and for many of our other developer tools as well.

speaker: Josh
text: Like the all-new Reality Composer Pro 3, which has been completely rebuilt for

speaker: Josh
text: crafting production-ready 3D experiences using RealityKit. It brings support for character animations, more realistic lighting,

speaker: Josh
text: and live previews that let you see the results of your edits as you make them using Mac Virtual Display.

speaker: Josh
text: And there's even more for game developers in this year's releases, including a major update to Game Porting Toolkit,

speaker: Josh
text: which dramatically cuts the time it takes to bring games to Apple platforms by adding AI skills for coding agents.

speaker: Josh
text: And new Metal command line tools give agents direct control during development and debugging, bringing best practices

speaker: Josh
text: for game development on Apple platforms to every step of your porting journey.

speaker: Josh
text: So that's developer productivity. Across the 2027 releases, there are so many new capabilities to build on,

speaker: Josh
text: like the App Intents framework, which lets you connect your app to Apple Intelligence, and the Foundation Models framework

speaker: Josh
text: and Core AI, which enable you to bring powerful generative intelligence features directly into your apps.

speaker: Josh
text: There are platform improvements across design, Swift, and SwiftUI that make your apps faster, more flexible, and easier to build.

speaker: Josh
text: And Xcode has even more expansive support for agentic coding.

speaker: Josh
text: and we've only just scratched the surface. There are over 100 sessions to dive deep into everything we've covered today,

speaker: Josh
text: including Apple Intelligence, Xcode 27, Design, and more.

speaker: Josh
text: All these sessions are available on the Apple Developer app, the website, YouTube,

speaker: Josh
text: and new this year on Bilibili. And there's so much more happening online throughout the week.

speaker: Josh
text: Sign up for Group Labs, online panels, and Q sessions with Apple engineers and designers.

speaker: Josh
text: And connect with us on the Apple Developer Forums to ask questions, and follow the conversation about the latest tools and technologies.

speaker: Josh
text: And the opportunities to connect extend well beyond this week. You can Meet with Apple around the world and online in hands-on workshops, labs,

speaker: Josh
text: and events to learn and connect as a community throughout the year.

speaker: Josh
text: We love when we can meet you in person. And there are many opportunities to do that in our Developer Centers,

speaker: Josh
text: located in Cupertino, Shanghai, Singapore, and Bengaluru.

speaker: Josh
text: And we're excited to announce the opening of our fifth this fall in Berlin,

speaker: Josh
text: home to one of Europe's most vibrant developer and designer communities. We can't wait to see you there or in one of our events online.

speaker: Josh
text: Whether this is your first WWDC or your 25th, thank you.

speaker: Josh
text: Your work inspires and drives us. We use the apps and play the games that you all build.

speaker: Josh
text: So, our greatest hope is that everything we talked about today will enable your next great idea to come to life.

speaker: Josh
text: We can't wait to see what you do next. Enjoy WWDC.
